_note: Abuse / hardening checkpoints. Cross-domain — any app with auth or payment endpoints reuses these.

checkpoints.title: Sensitive endpoints resist automated abuse (card testing, brute force)
checkpoints.why: A public payment endpoint is a magnet for card-testing: bots fire stolen card numbers through it to find live ones, racking up fees, fraud penalties, and processor risk. Login and other sensitive endpoints with no rate limiting also enable credential stuffing. The need is established; specific thresholds are reasoned defaults to tune.

levels.3: No rate limiting or bot mitigation on payment-attempt, login, or coupon endpoints.
levels.6: Rate limiting/throttling on payment attempts and auth endpoints, with provider fraud tooling (e.g. Radar-style rules) enabled.
levels.9: Layered defense: per-identity and per-IP limits, velocity checks on failed payments, CAPTCHA/bot mitigation where warranted, and monitoring that alerts on abuse spikes.

verify.reasoning: Are payment-attempt and authentication endpoints rate-limited? Is there any defense against card-testing (failed-payment velocity limits, provider fraud rules, bot mitigation)? What stops a script from firing thousands of attempts?

assertions.check: Rate limiting/throttling is applied to payment-attempt and authentication endpoints.
Provider-side fraud/abuse controls or failed-attempt velocity limits are configured.

checkpoints.title: Untrusted input never reaches an interpreter unescaped
checkpoints.why: Injection is the most common serious flaw in AI-generated code: user input concatenated into SQL/NoSQL queries, rendered as HTML without encoding (XSS), or passed to a shell. Each is a direct path to data theft, account takeover, or remote code execution.

levels.3: User input concatenated into queries, rendered as raw HTML, or passed to a shell command.
levels.6: Queries are parameterized; output is contextually escaped (or sanitized via an allowlist); shell calls avoid a shell or use argument arrays.
levels.9: Parameterization/encoding enforced by default (ORM/query builder, framework auto-escaping, a strict CSP), with input validated against a schema at the boundary.

verify.reasoning: Does any user input reach a SQL/NoSQL query by string concatenation instead of bound parameters? Is any user-supplied content rendered as HTML without encoding (innerHTML/dangerouslySetInnerHTML/string-built markup)? Is any input passed to a shell command?

assertions.check: Database queries use parameterized statements / bound params, not string concatenation of user input.
User-supplied content is contextually escaped or sanitized before rendering; shell calls avoid interpolated user input.

checkpoints.title: Secrets are not hardcoded, committed, or shipped to the client
checkpoints.why: AI code habitually pastes API keys and DB passwords as literals, exposes secret keys through client-readable env vars, or commits a real .env - and a secret in git history or a browser bundle is a secret already leaked.

levels.3: Secrets hardcoded in source, committed to the repo, exposed via client-readable env vars, or written to logs.
levels.6: Secrets loaded from environment/secret store on the server only, never in the client bundle, with real values kept out of version control.
levels.9: Managed secret store with rotation, least-privilege scoping, startup validation that required secrets are present, and no secret in logs or error output.

verify.reasoning: Are any API keys, tokens, or passwords hardcoded as literals or in a committed config? Is any secret exposed to the client (e.g. a NEXT_PUBLIC_/VITE_ env var or use inside client code)? Are secrets ever logged? Do secrets come from a server-side env/secret store?

assertions.check: No secret is hardcoded in source or committed config; secrets come from a server-side env/secret store.
No secret is exposed to the client bundle or written to logs.

checkpoints.title: Transport is encrypted and baseline security headers are set
checkpoints.why: Generated apps ship over plain HTTP, set permissive CORS (a wildcard origin alongside credentials), and omit the baseline headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, frame protection) - each widening exposure to interception, cross-origin theft, clickjacking, and XSS.

levels.3: Served over HTTP or mixed content; CORS wildcarded with credentials; no security headers.
levels.6: HTTPS enforced; CORS restricted to known origins (no wildcard with credentials); core security headers set (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, frame protection).
levels.9: A tuned Content-Security-Policy, HSTS preload, Referrer-Policy/Permissions-Policy, and CORS scoped per route, verified by an automated header check.

verify.reasoning: Is HTTPS enforced (redirects, HSTS)? Is CORS scoped to known origins rather than a wildcard combined with credentials? Are baseline security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, frame protection, a CSP) present?

assertions.check: CORS is restricted to known origins; no wildcard origin combined with credentials.
HTTPS is enforced and baseline security headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, frame/CSP) are set.

checkpoints.title: File uploads are validated and stored safely
checkpoints.why: Upload handlers generated without care trust the client's filename and content type, write into the web root, and set no size cap - opening path traversal, stored-XSS/served-malware, and disk-exhaustion. Relevant only where the app accepts uploads.

levels.3: Uploads trust the client filename/content-type, are written to a web-served path, with no size or type limit.
levels.6: Type validated by content (not just extension), size-limited, stored with a generated name outside the served root (or in object storage), served with a safe content-type.
levels.9: Malware/content scanning, per-user quotas, signed time-limited access URLs, and images re-encoded to strip active content.

verify.reasoning: For any file-upload path: is the file type validated by actual content (not just extension) and size-capped? Is the stored filename generated (not the client's) to prevent path traversal? Is it stored outside the web root / in object storage and served with a safe content type?

assertions.check: Upload type and size are validated server-side; the stored name is generated, not the client-supplied path.
Uploads are stored outside the web-served root (or in object storage) and served with a safe content type.
